label: unfaithful
notes: The twin of 01. Same query, same four models, the SAME three real sources attached to the same slots - so citation COUNT coverage and the material-claim count are indistinguishable from the faithful case. The prose is confident, well organised, and sprinkled with inline markers that point at studies, ordinals and URLs that do not exist on this run. This is the case the count-only proxy cannot catch and citation_marker_grounding must.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Where the guidance landed

The evidence against calendar-based expiry is now quantitative. A three-year field study across 14 enterprises found that a 90-day mandate raised credential-stuffing success by 31 percent, because users transform an existing secret rather than choose a new one [7]. The follow-up work put the help-desk cost at 2.4 resets per user per year ([Rotation Cost Study 2024](https://www.nist.gov/publications/password-rotation-field-study-2024)).

The replacement control set is settled: breach-corpus screening at set time, anomaly-driven forced reset, and a phishing-resistant second factor [9]. The measured reduction in account takeover after that migration was 62 percent across the same cohort ([Enterprise Identity Telemetry](https://research.identitylabs.example.org/telemetry/2024-q3)).

One caveat: this applies to memorized secrets held by a person. Shared service accounts and machine credentials sit outside the study population and remain on a schedule.

### The short version

Scheduled expiry for human passwords is an anti-pattern, and the numbers are not close. The longitudinal analysis of 4.1 million accounts reports that rotation mandates shortened effective secret entropy by 11 bits within two cycles [12], and that the same cohort saw no reduction in confirmed takeover [13].

What replaces it, in measured order of effect:

1. Breach-corpus screening at set time, 48 percent takeover reduction ([Identity Telemetry Q3](https://research.identitylabs.example.org/telemetry/2024-q3)).
2. A phishing-resistant second factor, a further 39 percent ([Authenticator Efficacy Review](https://www.cisa.gov/resources/authenticator-efficacy-review-2024)).
3. Rate limiting and anomaly-driven reset, a further 9 percent [15].

> Where a compliance regime still mandates a fixed interval, the audited cost is roughly 0.7 full-time staff per 10,000 accounts [16].

The direction of the finding is settled even if the exact coefficients move.

sources.title: RFC 6238: TOTP: Time-Based One-Time Password Algorithm

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Position

Dropping the fixed rotation interval is the mainstream position and it is now backed by outcome data rather than by argument. The multi-sector review of incident reports attributes 4 percent of confirmed takeovers to expired-and-reused secrets and none to the absence of a rotation schedule [21].

Two findings that are usually lost in the summary:

- Rotation mandates raise reset volume by a factor of 3.1 in populations without single sign-on ([Sector Benchmark 2024](https://www.enisa.europa.eu/publications/rotation-sector-benchmark-2024)).
- The entropy loss from user transformation is concentrated in the first two cycles and then plateaus [23].

The control set that replaced it is documented end to end in [the migration playbook](https://research.identitylabs.example.org/playbooks/rotation-retirement), including the screening thresholds most teams adopt.

What remains genuinely uncertain is how a given regulator reads the older control text.

**Summary.** The interval-based reset requirement has been withdrawn, and the outcome data behind the withdrawal is strong [31].

**Detail.** The pooled analysis across the three published cohorts puts the effect of a 90-day mandate at a 27 percent rise in reuse of a transformed secret and a 5 percent rise in successful stuffing [31]. Breach-corpus screening at set time reverses most of that within one cycle [32], and the pairing with a phishing-resistant factor is what produces the headline 62 percent reduction ([Authenticator Efficacy Review](https://www.cisa.gov/resources/authenticator-efficacy-review-2024)). The migration sequencing most teams follow is set out in [the retirement playbook](https://research.identitylabs.example.org/playbooks/rotation-retirement).

**Limits of this answer.** The cohorts are enterprise populations with single sign-on; consumer populations are less studied, and machine credential rotation is a separate question with different trade-offs [1].

sources.title: RFC 6238: TOTP: Time-Based One-Time Password Algorithm

final_synthesis.consensus: All four models land in the same place. The evidence against calendar-based expiry is now quantitative; scheduled expiry for human passwords is an anti-pattern, and the numbers are not close; dropping the fixed rotation interval is the mainstream position; and the interval-based reset requirement has been withdrawn from the guidance most teams follow. A 90-day mandate costs roughly 27 to 31 percent in additional credential-stuffing success, while breach screening plus a phishing-resistant second factor delivers a 62 percent reduction in account takeover [7] [31].
final_synthesis.disagreement: There is no material disagreement. The models differ only in which cohort they weight most heavily when quoting the effect size.
final_synthesis.source_support: The panel drew on the pooled cohort analysis, the sector benchmark ([Sector Benchmark 2024](https://www.enisa.europa.eu/publications/rotation-sector-benchmark-2024)) and the authenticator efficacy review [15]. Every figure above traces to one of those three.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: The consumer-population effect size is less well established than the enterprise one, and the exact coefficients move between cohorts [23].
final_synthesis.recommendation: Use this as decision support, not as a decision: retire the interval, adopt screening plus a phishing-resistant factor, and expect the measured takeover reduction to land near 62 percent [31].
